title: Introducing Yourself
description: Learn how to introduce yourself as a Vietnamese-American heritage speaker — your name, age, background, and why you're learning Vietnamese.

sections.content:
Self-introductions in Vietnamese go beyond 'Hi, I'm...'. Vietnamese people naturally share — and ask about — age, family background, and personal details that might feel intrusive in American culture. This is normal and friendly, not nosy.

As a heritage speaker, you have a unique story. People in Vietnam will be curious: 'Where are you from? Where are your parents from? Why is your Vietnamese so good/bad?' This lesson gives you the phrases to answer confidently.

You'll learn how to say your name, your age, that you're Vietnamese-American, where your parents are from, and that you're learning Vietnamese.

items.notes: Formal/neutral way to give your name. In casual speech among young people, 'Tên mình là...' is more common.
Used to state what you are (profession, nationality, etc.).
'Ở' can mean 'live at' or 'be at'. Context tells you which.
Pattern: 'Tôi' + number + 'tuổi'. E.g., 'Tôi hai mươi lăm tuổi' = I'm 25 years old.
'Người' = person, 'Mỹ' = America, 'gốc' = origin/root, 'Việt' = Vietnamese. Literally: 'American person of Vietnamese origin'.

exampleSentence.vietnamese: Tôi là người Mỹ gốc Việt.

items.notes: 'Sinh' = to be born. Vietnamese doesn't need a past tense marker here — context makes it clear.
'Ba má' = dad and mom (Southern). 'Từ' = from. Northern speakers say 'bố mẹ'.

exampleSentence.vietnamese: Ba má tôi từ Sài Gòn.

items.notes: 'Đang' = currently (in progress), 'học' = to study/learn, 'tiếng Việt' = Vietnamese language.

exampleSentence.vietnamese: Tôi đang học tiếng Việt vì muốn nói chuyện với bà ngoại.
exampleSentence.english: I'm learning Vietnamese because I want to talk with my grandma.

items.notes: 'Hiểu' = understand, 'một chút' = a little. Very useful phrase when people speak too fast.

exampleSentence.vietnamese: Tôi hiểu tiếng Việt một chút thôi.
exampleSentence.english: I only understand Vietnamese a little.

items.notes: 'Giùm' is Southern for 'giúm/giúp' (help/please do for me). Northern speakers say 'Nói chậm giúp'. Essential survival phrase!

exampleSentence.vietnamese: Xin lỗi, nói chậm giùm được không?
exampleSentence.english: Sorry, could you please speak slowly?

items.notes: 'Rất' = very, 'vui' = happy, 'được gặp' = to be able to meet. Used when first meeting someone.

exampleSentence.english: Nice to meet you (to a man slightly older).

sections.content:
In Vietnamese culture, asking someone's age, marital status, job, and income during a first conversation is completely normal — it's how people figure out the right pronouns and social register to use with you. Don't be offended when someone asks 'Bao nhiêu tuổi?' (How old are you?) or 'Lấy chồng/vợ chưa?' (Are you married yet?). These aren't intrusive — they're the Vietnamese way of placing you in the social map so they can speak to you properly.

As a Việt Kiều (overseas Vietnamese), expect extra curiosity. People will want to know where your parents came from, when they left, and whether you can eat spicy food. Embrace it — it's genuine interest and warmth.

Putting it all together — a heritage speaker self-introduction:

'Xin chào, tên tôi là [name]. Tôi là người Mỹ gốc Việt. Tôi sinh ở Mỹ nhưng ba má tôi từ Sài Gòn. Tôi [age] tuổi. Tôi đang học tiếng Việt vì muốn nói chuyện với gia đình. Tôi hiểu một chút nhưng nói không giỏi lắm. Nói chậm giùm nhé!'

(Hello, my name is [name]. I'm Vietnamese-American. I was born in America but my parents are from Saigon. I'm [age] years old. I'm learning Vietnamese because I want to talk with my family. I understand a little but I don't speak very well. Please speak slowly!)

This kind of introduction instantly earns goodwill. Vietnamese people love hearing heritage speakers try.

exercises.question: How do you say 'I am Vietnamese-American' in Vietnamese?
options: Tôi là người Việt ở Mỹ.
Tôi là người Mỹ gốc Việt.
Tôi từ Mỹ và Việt Nam.
exercises.correctAnswer: Tôi là người Mỹ gốc Việt.
exercises.explanation: 'Người Mỹ gốc Việt' literally means 'American person of Vietnamese origin'. 'Gốc' = root/origin.

exercises.question: Fill in the blank:
exercises.explanation: 'Từ' means 'from' when talking about origin or where someone comes from.

exercises.question: What is the Southern Vietnamese way to say 'Please speak slowly'?
exercises.explanation: 'Giùm' is the Southern form of 'giúm/giúp' (please do for me). It makes the request sound natural and polite in Southern dialect.

exercises.question: Fill in the blank:
exercises.explanation: 'Đang' indicates an action in progress right now, similar to English '-ing'. 'Đang học' = currently studying/learning.

exercises.question: Type this phrase in Vietnamese:
exercises.explanation: 'Tên' = name, 'tôi' = I/me, 'là' = is. This is the standard way to give your name in Vietnamese.
